title: The Space Race Comes to Latin America - Americas Quarterly
author: JUAN PABLO TORO
text:
Our Lead EditorialView PDFPast IssuesThe Space Race Comes to Latin AmericaThe region wants to be not just a battleground, but a protagonist in the burgeoning space economy.By Juan Pablo Toro |

Latin America’s Quest for Space SovereigntyThe region’s pursuit of autonomy in the growing space sector will be complicated. The U.S. can be an important partner.By Laura Delgado López | The Crucial Challenge of Building Space ExpertiseAs Latin America acquires more space technology, workforce development will be key to reaping the benefits.By Jeanette Nuñez |

TAKING FLIGHTInitiatives from Brazil, Argentina and Mexico show how the space industry is getting off the ground, although numerous challenges remain.Latin America Has a Space Agency. Now Comes the Hard PartALCE held its first general assembly in Mexico in February, but faces questions about funding and political buy-in.By Cyntia Barrera Díaz | How Satellogic Is Trying to Reshape the Space RaceThe company founded in Buenos Aires is pushing competitors to rethink who owns the sky.By Horacio Aizpeolea | Brazil’s Landmark Satellite ProgramAmazônia-1, the first satellite entirely designed and operated by Brazil, is part of a larger mission.By Emilie Sweigart | Argentina’s Global Satellite AspirationsThe national space agency’s satellite program is a rare example of sustained strategic investment.By Horacio Aizpeolea |

Latin America’s Final FrontierThe booming space sector offers opportunities, if the region can seize them.By Susan Segal | Despite Appearances, Climate Has Not Disappeared from the AgendaThe issue is more persistent than it seems, emerging in other topics like crime and mining, writes a former president.By Carlos Alvarado Quesada | When the Cuban Missile Crisis Shook the HemisphereAs nuclear war loomed, ordinary people throughout Latin America made their voices heard.By Renata Keller | The Problem with Latin America’s Prison BoomGovernments are building massive facilities to contain a growing inmate population, but the strategy could backfire.By Robert Muggah | Why the Liberal Order Is Unraveling in the AmericasCitizens and leaders are abandoning the rules of liberal democracy. What can reverse the trend?By Andrés Malamud, Juan Pablo Luna, Alberto Vergara | Q&A: Thomas Traumann on Brazil’s October ElectionsA veteran political analyst discusses voter trends and the issues that will shape the Lula-Flávio showdown.By Emilie Sweigart |

CulturaThe Hidden History of São Paulo’s “Japanese” NeighborhoodA new book shows how Brazil’s biggest city often literally paved over its Black past.By Brian Winter | Greek Mythology and Mexican Corridos Collide in a Startling Short Story CollectionDahlia de la Cerda’s latest book takes on what it means to be a woman living through Mexico’s security crisis.By Daniela Ordoñez Delgado | Frida and Diego Get New Life in New YorkA collaboration between The Met Opera and The Museum of Modern Art gives the famed Mexican artists a fresh treatment.By Miranda Mazariegos | A New Documentary Chronicles the Decay of Small-Town CubaThe filmmaker returned home to find the town he grew up in disappearing, even as its artists continue to build a legacy.By Ena Alvarado | Opera’s Many Faces in Latin AmericaAQ’s music columnist traces the paths of a genre that thrived in Latin America, from its colonial courts and Jesuit missions to its revolutions.By Sebastián Zubieta |
